meter_definition: For each submitted pack, the Workbench component 3 (Artefact Detector) automatically parses the submission and maps each artefact (ConOps, SORA, Safety Case, Training Plan, Personnel List, Environmental Assessment, Insurance, Ground Survey, crew credentials, ML manifest if applicable) against a regulatory artefact library. For each artefact class, the system records: presence/absence, file format validation (PDF/XLSX/DOCX), and page count. A pack passes the Quick Gate if all 8 mandatory artefact types are present, validly formatted, and exceed minimum page thresholds (ConOps >= 5 pages, SORA >= 3, Safety Case >= 10). Run on every submission within 10 minutes of upload. Output: RAG matrix (Red = missing artefact, Amber = present but below threshold, Green = present and sufficient). Triggers manual review if any Amber or Red flags.
measurement_tool: Workbench Artefact Detector component (automated file parser + clause mapper); RAG matrix dashboard
measurement_owner: Workbench engine (automated); CAA case officer reviews exceptions
reasoning: Per Planguage guidance, the Meter is the measurement process, not the Scale. This Meter operationalizes PackCompleteness by running automated presence/absence detection on each artefact class at intake. It's fast, cheap, and credible because it delegates to the Workbench's existing parsing capability. Accuracy is high for file presence; precision is high because the same detector always produces the same output. Confidence: 9.

meter_definition: After Quick Gate passes, a detailed audit runs on each artefact: for each required section (identified from regulatory checklist per operation type [OpType]), human or semi-automated reviewer verifies that the section contains substantive content, not placeholder text. For ConOps, mandatory sections are: (1) Swarm composition and roles, (2) Operational phases with crew triggers, (3) Failure modes and contingency responses, (4) Worst-credible scenarios and crew procedures. For SORA: (1) Loss-of-separation hazard tree, (2) Swarm-specific interdependency evidence, (3) Risk mitigation controls. Reviewer marks each section: Present & Substantive (green), Present But Vague (amber), Absent (red). Section-level coverage target: >= 90% green. Records paragraph pointers (page, line range) for traceability. Run once per case, triggered after Quick Gate passes, before SORA assessment begins.
measurement_tool: Clause-by-clause checklist (Workbench or manual audit spreadsheet); semi-automated text classifier (detect placeholder keywords: 'TBD', 'to be determined', 'TBC', single-sentence sections); human reviewer scoring sheet
measurement_owner: CAA case officer + independent contract auditor (mixed human-in-the-loop); Workbench engine screens for obvious gaps
reasoning: PackCompleteness has two sub-scales: Quick (automated file presence) and Thorough (substantive content). This Meter captures the thorough, costly path. Accuracy is medium because 'substantive' is subjective; precision is medium for the same reason. Cost is medium (2-4 hours for full audit). Credibility is high because independent human audit builds stakeholder confidence. Confidence: 8.

meter_definition: SORA assessor reviews the submitted SORA evidence document and extracts all risk items (hazards, failure modes, controls, residual risks). For each risk item, assessor tags whether it explicitly addresses swarm-specific failure modes: (1) Coordinated-link-loss cascades (e.g., one aircraft's C2 loss triggers loss-of-separation within swarm), (2) Emergent misbehavior (e.g., consensus deadlock, oscillation), (3) Unintended inter-vehicle interactions (e.g., RF interference, wake effects). Assessor records: Risk ID, SORA hazard category, swarm-specific tag (Yes/No), evidence confidence (High/Medium/Low). Target: >= 3 distinct swarm-aware risk items per aircraft, distributed across the three categories above. Count is target metric; each item must cite supporting evidence (test report, design review memo, simulation data). Run once per case after Quick Gate, before SAIL derivation.
measurement_tool: SORA risk-item extraction checklist; swarm-specific tag taxonomy; evidence traceability log (hyperlinks to supporting docs)
reasoning: The Scale (SwarmAwareSORAEvidence) requires count of swarm-aware risk items. The Meter is the process of extracting, tagging, and counting. Accuracy is medium because swarm-aware vs. single-aircraft interpretation requires domain judgment. Precision is medium for the same reason. Cost is medium (3-5 hours for full SORA review). Credibility is high because SORA assessor signature carries regulatory weight. Confidence: 8.

meter_definition: Two independent safety-case reviewers (neither has seen ConOps before) are given: (1) ConOps document, (2) ConOps Clarity Scoring Sheet (structured questionnaire: 'For takeoff phase, describe nominal behavior boundary in 2-3 sentences' + 'For takeoff phase, what is worst-credible behavior?' + 10 similar phase-by-phase questions). Each reviewer independently scores clarity 0-100 per phase. Answers are recorded verbatim. After both review, answers are compared. Cohen's kappa is calculated on structured questions (ordinal scale: Absent=0, Vague=1, Clear=2). ConOps passes Clarity Meter if: (1) kappa >= 0.80 across all phases (high inter-rater agreement), (2) Both reviewers score >= 80/100 overall clarity. Cost to run: 4-6 hours (2-3 hours per reviewer). Run once per case before Geofence/DAA assurance begins.
measurement_tool: Structured clarity scoring sheet (questionnaire + ordinal scale); kappa calculator; independent reviewer pairing process
measurement_owner: CAA safety-case reviewers (2x independent domain experts); coordinating case officer tabulates results
reasoning: ConOpsClarity's sub-scale is ReviewerAgreement (inter-rater reliability, kappa >= 0.8). This Meter is the explicit dual-review consensus test. Accuracy and precision are high because kappa is a mathematically well-defined inter-rater metric. Cost is medium (multi-reviewer time). Credibility is high because it's objective and reproducible. Confidence: 8.

meter_definition: Before first flight, Range Safety Officer (RSO) or Workbench simulator runs 10,000 Monte Carlo trials of geofence enforcement. Each trial: (1) Initializes swarm in random position within authorized area, (2) Simulates random failure modes from list: C2 loss, GPS loss, altimeter failure, geofence logic fault, single-aircraft power loss. (3) Runs geofence logic at 10Hz, (4) Checks if aircraft exceeds boundary by >= 50m (pre-configured tolerance). Success = aircraft recovers to within boundary or triggers RTL before 50m breach. Target: >= 99.9% of trials show zero boundary exceedance. Outputs: pass/fail heatmap (boundary hot spots), distribution of boundary-approach distances, time-to-containment histograms. If pass, geofence is cleared for flight. If fail, airborne limits are tightened and sim re-runs. Cost: 2-4 hours for full 10k trial suite on modern hardware.
measurement_tool: Workbench simulator (Monte Carlo engine) or independent flight-dynamics simulator (e.g., jMAVSim, Gazebo); trial data logger (CSV output)
measurement_frequency: per-submission + event-triggered (re-run after any geofence code change)
measurement_owner: Workbench engine (automated) + Range Safety Officer (human sign-off on sim assumptions)
reasoning: GeofenceContainment Scale specifies 99.9% success rate across Monte Carlo trials. This Meter IS the simulation gauntlet. Accuracy and precision are high because simulator is deterministic and repeatable. Cost is medium (compute time). Credibility is high because it's transparent and auditable. This is load-bearing: evidence of geofence containment before first flight is non-negotiable for safety. Confidence: 9.

meter_definition: After Monte Carlo passes, a controlled flight test is conducted with Range Safety Officer present and termination system armed. Setup: (1) Swarm launches within authorized area. (2) RSO deliberately commands aircraft to approach boundary via GPS waypoint or C2 manual input. (3) Geofence logic is monitored in real-time. (4) Test measures: time from boundary approach alert to RTL trigger, distance at RTL initiation, post-RTL containment time. Test data: 5 deliberate approach attempts per flight, 3-5 flights. Success = all approaches trigger RTL before boundary exceedance; containment time measured in seconds. This is a fly-away stress test; it validates that the geofence actually works under real conditions (GPS noise, latency, wind, etc.) not just in simulator. Cost: 1-2 days of range time, RSO supervision, safety equipment. Run once per operation type before first autonomous flight.
measurement_tool: Live airborne GPS receiver + geofence monitor log; RSO observation + video record; post-flight telemetry analysis
measurement_frequency: per-review + event-triggered (re-run after major swarm or geofence redesign)
measurement_owner: Range Safety Officer (human); Workbench logs data; independent post-flight analysis
reasoning: This Meter is the live fly-away test specified in guidance. It's the expensive, thorough validation that geofence containment is real. Accuracy and precision are high because you're measuring actual aircraft behavior. Cost is high (range time, personnel). Speed is low (days). Credibility is VERY high because it demonstrates working hardware + software integration. Justified cost: geofence is load-bearing safety-critical for BVLOS swarm ops. Confidence: 9.

meter_definition: Controlled simulation test: swarm is flying autonomous waypoint mission. Test operator cuts C2 link (RF kill switch or simulator command). Telemetry system logs: (1) Link loss detection time (latency from actual link break to system recognizing loss), (2) Swarm response: do swarm members continue waypoint navigation? Do inter-vehicle links maintain cohesion? (3) Survival duration: how long before swarm enters unsafe state (loss-of-separation, uncontrolled descent, etc.). Test runs 10 scenarios with link-loss duration ranging from 100ms to 30+ seconds. Targets: (a) Autonomous survival >= 30 seconds for loss-of-C2 only, (b) Crew takeover latency <= 5 seconds (time for crew to see alert and manually intervene), (c) Inter-vehicle link-loss recovery within 10 seconds. Data logged: link loss duration, survival time, separation maintained, crew reaction time. Run in simulation first (10 trials, 1 hour), then in live flight test (3-5 sorties, 1-2 days).
measurement_tool: Workbench simulator (C2 link-loss injection) + live flight test with RF kill switch; telemetry logger; separation monitoring (ADS-B or inter-vehicle ranging)
measurement_frequency: per-submission + per-review + event-triggered (re-test after link architecture change)
measurement_owner: Workbench engine (sim); Range Safety Officer + flight test crew (live); data analyst
reasoning: C2LinkResilience Scale specifies time thresholds (30s autonomous, 120s with crew). This Meter is the hold-safe survival test. Accuracy is high because timings are measured objectively. Precision is high because scenarios are repeatable. Cost is medium (sim + flight time). Credibility is high because it's grounded in both simulation and live flight. Confidence: 8.

meter_definition: DAA system is tested against a suite of >= 100 encounter scenarios drawn from DO-365 (Detect-and-Avoid standards). Scenarios span: (1) Cooperative intruders (ADS-B equipped, altitude/velocity known), (2) Non-cooperative intruders (radar-only, no velocity info), (3) Head-on, crossing, overtake geometries. For each scenario: (1) Intruder is injected into swarm flight path, (2) DAA system detects intruder and initiates resolution, (3) System logs: detection latency, resolution maneuver (altitude change, lateral displacement, speed change), outcome (separation achieved, secondary conflict, miss). Target metrics: (a) >= 95% of encounters result in recovery to >= 500ft horizontal, >= 100ft vertical separation, (b) Recovery achieved within [RecoveryTime], typically 60 seconds, (c) No secondary conflicts (re-engagement with same or new intruder). Decision Consistency sub-scale: same encounter scenario repeated 3 times; DAA decision must be identical >= 95% of trials (determinism check). Run in simulation first (100 scenarios, 2 hours compute time), then subset in live flight test (10-15 encounters per sortie, 3-5 flights).
measurement_tool: DO-365 test scenario database (100 pre-built encounter geometries); Workbench simulator with intruder injection + DAA monitor; live flight test with chase aircraft or simulated intruder track injection
measurement_frequency: per-submission + per-review + event-triggered (re-test after DAA logic change)
measurement_owner: Workbench engine (sim) + qualified DAA tester (live); independent analysis
reasoning: DAAPerformance Scale specifies encounter resolution success rate, recovery time, decision consistency. This Meter is the DO-365 test suite. Accuracy is high (separation measured objectively). Precision is high (deterministic scenarios). Cost is medium (compute + flight time). Credibility is VERY high because DO-365 is an external, recognized standard. Confidence: 9.

meter_definition: Autonomy designer submits a structured manifest of emergent behaviors identified in the swarm architecture. For each behavior (e.g., formation tightening, consensus deadlock, flocking), the manifest documents: (1) Description of the behavior (what happens, when does it occur), (2) Quantified boundary (state space limits, e.g., 'formation tolerance +/- 10m', 'consensus latency <= 2 seconds'), (3) Detection mechanism ('if inter-aircraft separation drops below 5m, trigger alert'), (4) Recovery action ('expand formation limits by 20%, reset consensus algorithm'). Auditor reviews manifest and verifies: (1) >= 5 distinct emergent behaviors identified, (2) Each behavior has a quantified boundary (not vague), (3) Each behavior has a defined detection + recovery mechanism. Targets: >= 5 behaviors, 100% with boundaries, 100% with detection/recovery. Cost: 2-3 hours for full audit. Run once per case before autonomous flight begins.
measurement_tool: Emergent Behavior Manifest template (structured form); auditor checklist (presence/absence of boundaries, detection, recovery)
reasoning: SwarmEmergentBoundedness Scale requires >=5 emergent behaviors with quantified boundaries. This Meter is the documentation audit. Accuracy is medium because 'emergent' is subjective; precision is medium for the same reason. Cost is low (desk audit). Speed is high. Credibility is medium because it's documentation-based; actual test evidence follows in METER-010. Confidence: 7.

meter_name: Emergent Behavior Flight Test Coverage Validator
meter_definition: During flight testing, telemetry logs capture swarm state at 10Hz or higher. After each flight, data is analyzed: for each emergent behavior from the manifest (METER-009), auditor verifies: (1) Did the behavior occur during flight? If yes, did it stay within documented boundary? (2) If boundary was exceeded, was detection mechanism triggered? (3) Was recovery action effective? Cumulative metric: percentage of flight-hours where swarm operated within all documented emergent-behavior boundaries. Target: >= 95% of test flight hours show in-bounds behavior. Threshold: if >= 1 out-of-bounds event occurs in 5 flight-hours, the behavior boundary is re-tightened and additional test hours are required. Data logged: timestamp, behavior trigger event, state (in-bounds vs. out-of-bounds), recovery outcome. Cost: 1 hour per flight for data analysis + 0.5 day for cumulative trend analysis. Run continuously throughout phased trial program (phases 2-4).
measurement_tool: Flight telemetry parser (extract swarm state); emergent behavior boundary checker (automated comparison); trend analysis dashboard
measurement_owner: Workbench engine (automated parsing) + CAA autonomy reviewer (human interpretation); Range Safety Officer monitors real-time alerts
reasoning: SwarmEmergentBoundedness Scale requires test evidence (>= 95% flight hours in-bounds). This Meter is the post-flight analysis. Accuracy is high because boundaries are objective limits. Precision is high (same analyzer always produces same result on same data). Cost is low (automated parsing + human review). Speed is high. Credibility is high because it's grounded in real flight data. Confidence: 8.

meter_definition: For each safety-critical ML model (e.g., autonomy-mode classifier, emergency-decision network), a post-hoc forensic test is conducted: (1) Select N=50 representative decisions from logged flight data. (2) For each decision, freeze the model state at that moment (inputs, weights, internal activations if available). (3) Provide explainability evidence (model type dependent): decision-tree = decision path (unambiguous), SVM = feature importance + decision boundary visualization, neural network = saliency map + LIME/SHAP explanation. (4) Ask two independent domain experts (not the model developers): 'Given the explainability evidence, do you agree this decision makes sense?' Score: agrees (1), partially agrees (0.5), disagrees (0). (5) Target: >= 80% of decisions scored as agrees/partially-agrees by both reviewers. Cost: 2-3 hours per model. Run before first autonomous flight; repeat after model retraining.
measurement_tool: Model decision log (timestamp + inputs + decision), frozen model state snapshot, explainability framework (LIME/SHAP for neural networks, feature importance for SVM, decision path for trees), independent reviewer scoring sheet
measurement_owner: ML assurance specialist (human) + domain expert reviewers; Workbench captures model state logs
reasoning: MLForensicExplainability Scale requires reviewer reconstruction of decisions. This Meter IS the forensic test. Accuracy is medium because expert judgment on 'makes sense' is subjective. Precision is medium for the same reason. Cost is medium (expert time). Credibility is high because independent reviewers + standardized evidence framework. Confidence: 8.

meter_name: Crew State-Update Frequency & Display Completeness Audit
meter_definition: Human factors inspector reviews ConOps and UI mockups/actual system. Verifies crew display updates swarm state at target frequency: nominal operations >= 2Hz (swarm position, health, altitude), off-nominal scenarios >= 10Hz (alerts, DAA events, link status). Inspector also audits display completeness: does crew see (1) individual aircraft state (position, velocity, altitude, health), (2) swarm-level metrics (formation geometry, inter-aircraft separation, consensus state), (3) external threats (detected intruders, geofence proximity, weather radar), (4) crew command authority (what decisions does crew control vs. autonomy?). Scoring rubric: 0-100 scale where 100 = full state visibility at required frequency, 0 = no display. Target: >= 80/100. Cost: 2 hours for audit. Run once before crew training begins.
measurement_tool: Display audit checklist (component list); frequency measurement (Hz counter in live system or simulator); human factors scoring rubric
measurement_owner: CAA human factors inspector (human domain expert)
reasoning: MeaningfulHumanControl sub-scale is SituationalAwarenessScore (0-100). This Meter is the display audit. Accuracy and precision are medium because display adequacy is subjective. Cost is low. Speed is high. Credibility is medium; the real test is whether crew performs well in simulation/flight (METER-013). Confidence: 7.

meter_name: Crew Workload & Takeover Latency Simulation Test
meter_definition: Crew is placed in high-fidelity simulator with full swarm flight scenario. Scenario unfolds: (1) Nominal transit (baseline workload measurement), (2) Unexpected event injection (e.g., DAA encounter, C2 glitch, swarm member health alert). During simulation: (1) Crew's workload is measured via NASA-TLX questionnaire at key points (target: workload <= 70% during nominal, <= 85% during events). (2) Takeover latency is measured: from time an alert is displayed to time crew issues manual command (target: <= 5 seconds). (3) Concurrent event load is counted: how many simultaneous alarms/alerts? (target: <= 3 concurrent). (4) Duty cycle is logged: total time crew is actively controlling aircraft (target: <= 6 minutes per hour). Test runs 5 scenarios, each 30-60 minutes. Composite score (0-100): weight as (50% workload) + (25% takeover latency) + (25% event load). Target: >= 80/100. Cost: 1-2 days for test design + crew prep + run + analysis. Run once before first flight; repeat if crew composition changes.
measurement_tool: High-fidelity flight simulator (visual, motion if available); NASA-TLX workload questionnaire; event-timing instrument (millisecond-level latency measurement); duty-cycle timer
measurement_owner: CAA human factors inspector (test designer + observer) + independent test pilot; Workbench simulator provides scenario execution
reasoning: MeaningfulHumanControl Scale specifies workload, takeover latency, concurrent events. This Meter is the simulation test. Accuracy is high because latencies and workload are objectively measured. Precision is medium because simulator fidelity varies. Cost is high (full simulator time). Speed is low (days). Credibility is VERY high because it's grounded in human factors standards (NASA-TLX) and realistic simulation. This is load-bearing: without evidence of adequate crew control, authorization cannot be granted. Confidence: 9.

meter_definition: Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) is conducted on termination system (hardware, firmware, power supply, sensors, command path). From FMEA, >= 50 failure scenarios are identified. Test procedure: (1) For each scenario, inject the failure (simulator or hardware-in-the-loop testbed). (2) Trigger an off-nominal condition (e.g., geofence breach detected, C2 loss, autonomy fault). (3) Measure: does termination system trigger correctly? Does termination execute (motors off, safe descent)? What is execution time? (4) Record success/failure. Target: >= 95% of scenarios result in successful termination within [TerminationTime] (e.g., 10 seconds from trigger to safe state). Environmental robustness: repeat test subset across environmental extremes (wind 0-25 kt, temperature -20 to +60°C, electromagnetic interference). Cost: 1-2 weeks for FMEA + 2-3 days for test execution. Run once before first flight; repeat after any termination system redesign.
measurement_tool: FMEA template (standard MIL-STD-1629A format); failure injection testbed (simulator or hardware test rig); timer (measure execution latency); environmental chamber (optional for robustness testing)
measurement_owner: Workbench engine (simulation) + Range Safety Officer (hardware testbed) + independent safety auditor (FMEA review)
reasoning: IndependentFlightTermination Scale specifies trigger reliability + environmental robustness. This Meter is the FMEA-derived fault-injection test. Accuracy is high (success measured objectively). Precision is high (repeatable scenarios). Cost is high (weeks of FMEA + test time). Speed is low. Credibility is VERY high because FMEA is a recognized safety methodology and fault-injection is industry-standard validation. This is CRITICAL load-bearing evidence: independent termination must work or swarm poses unacceptable risk. Confidence: 9.

meter_definition: Before flight commences, data recording system is audited: (1) Schema audit: verify all required state variables are logged (position XYZ, velocity, acceleration, heading, engine RPM, autonomy mode, sensor inputs, crew commands, system alerts). Target: all 15+ variables present. (2) Sampling rate audit: verify sampling frequency meets spec (>= 10Hz for nominal state, >= 50Hz for high-dynamics phases). (3) Data integrity audit: post-flight, a sample of logged data is checked for gaps > 100ms, checksums verified, timestamp continuity verified. Target: >= 99% of data intervals are gap-free and valid. (4) Forensic provenance: verify each data file has cryptographic hash, timestamp, source certification (e.g., 'aircraft-0-onboard-logger'). Cost: 2-3 hours for pre-flight audit, 1 hour per flight for post-flight integrity check. Run before every flight.
measurement_tool: Data schema checklist; data logger configuration parser; gap detector (automated script); checksum validator; audit log template
measurement_owner: Workbench engine (automated checks) + Flight Safety Officer (human review of integrity results)
reasoning: MORForensicCompleteness Scale requires complete recorded evidence (schema, sampling rate, integrity, provenance). This Meter is the pre/post-flight audit. Accuracy is high (objective checks). Precision is high. Cost is low (mostly automated). Speed is high. Credibility is high because it's objective and verifiable. Confidence: 8.

meter_definition: For each crew member and assigned role (pilot, sensor operator, safety officer), a simulator-based assessment is conducted. Scenarios test: (1) Link-loss response (correct execution of RTL / contingency procedures), (2) Geofence-breach response (abort mission, land immediately), (3) DAA encounter response (acknowledge alert, position swarm for safe separation), (4) Swarm-member health-alert response (remove member from formation, continue mission safely). Each scenario is run twice; crew must achieve >= 90% success rate (correct procedure, appropriate latency, no secondary errors). Scoring: pass/fail per procedure. Target: 100% of required procedures scored PASS. Cost: 4-6 hours per crew member (scenario prep + briefing + runs + debrief). Run once per crew member before authorization, then annually thereafter.
measurement_tool: High-fidelity flight simulator; procedure checklist (Go/No-Go scoring); video record (for replay/review); training records template
measurement_owner: CAA training inspector + qualified check pilot; Workbench simulator provides scenario execution
reasoning: CrewCompetencyEvidence Scale requires demonstrated proficiency in simulation (>= 90% success). This Meter is the emergency-procedure simulation test. Accuracy is high (pass/fail is objective). Precision is high (repeatable procedures). Cost is medium (simulator time). Credibility is high because simulation is a recognized training/validation method. Confidence: 8.

meter_definition: A responsibility matrix is constructed (rows = safety-critical design/operational domains from SORA hazard list + design domains + operational domains; columns = CAA responsibility, MAA responsibility, shared, unassigned). For each row, the applicant assigns responsibility to exactly one party (CAA, MAA, or explicitly shared with defined sub-responsibilities). Auditor verifies: (1) HazardCoverage: all SORA-identified hazards are assigned (count unassigned = target: 0). (2) DesignDomainCoverage: avionics design, swarm decision logic, autonomy limits, geofence system, termination system (count unassigned = target: 0). (3) OperationalDomainCoverage: airspace segregation, crew training, incident response, post-trial close-out (count unassigned = target: 0). (4) OverlapDetection: no domain is assigned to both CAA and MAA (count overlaps = target: 0). Signed by authorized CAA and MAA representatives. Cost: 3-4 hours for matrix construction + audit. Run before case opening.
measurement_tool: Responsibility matrix template (spreadsheet or structured form); traceability mapping (each domain -> assigned party); audit checklist (count gaps + overlaps)
reasoning: CAAMAAApportionmentGapFreeness Scale requires 100% coverage with no gaps/overlaps. This Meter is the responsibility matrix audit. Accuracy is high (coverage is countable). Precision is high. Cost is low (desk work). Speed is high. Credibility is high because it's verifiable and signed by both parties. Confidence: 8.

meter_name: Phased Gate Criteria & Reversibility Procedure Specification
meter_definition: For each trial phase (e.g., Phase 1: 2-aircraft VLOS, Phase 2: 4-aircraft VLOS, Phase 3: 4-aircraft BVLOS), a gate specification document is drafted. For each gate, the document contains: (1) Gate Criterion (quantified evidence threshold, e.g., 'Phase 1 gate: 10 successful flights with zero loss-of-separation incidents'). (2) Reversibility Procedure (if new evidence emerges that suggests unacceptable risk, how does trial exit the phase? E.g., 'If loss-of-separation event occurs, Phase 1 gate fails; trial reverts to ground testing'). (3) Data Immutability (all phase evidence is preserved in write-once storage or audit-logged so gates can be re-evaluated). (4) Re-entry Procedure (what additional evidence is required before re-attempting gate progression? E.g., '5 additional flights with new geofence design before Phase 2 re-try'). Auditor verifies: Gate count >= 4, 100% of gates have quantified criteria, 100% have documented exit/reversal procedures, data immutability confirmed. Cost: 2-3 hours for document review. Run once at trial start.
measurement_tool: Gate specification template (structured form); reversibility procedure checklist; data immutability verification (audit log + write-once storage config)
reasoning: PhasedGateReversibility Scale requires >= 4 gates with clear criteria and reversibility. This Meter is the gate specification audit. Accuracy is high (criteria are countable). Precision is high. Cost is low. Speed is high. Credibility is high because it's documented and verifiable. Confidence: 8.

meter_name: CAP 1616 Consultation Audit & Community Engagement Report
meter_definition: CAP 1616 (Engagement, Coordination and Access for Airspace Users) prescribes a consultation process. Meter: auditor reviews consultation evidence package submitted by applicant. Verifies: (1) StakeholderIdentification: applicant has identified >= 5 distinct stakeholder groups (residents within [Radius] km, landowners, environmental groups, aviation operators, media). (2) PriorNotification: >= 30 days advance notice issued to stakeholders before first flight. (3) FeedbackCollection: structured feedback mechanism (survey, workshop, forum) documented; track percentage of stakeholders responding. (4) FeedbackResponseCoverage: for each feedback item, applicant has documented response (acceptance, mitigation, or rejection with rationale). Target: >= 80% of feedback items receive documented response. Consultation log archived with case file. Cost: 3-4 hours for audit. Run before TDA approval.
measurement_tool: CAP 1616 consultation checklist; stakeholder contact log; feedback collection records (survey results, meeting minutes); response log (spreadsheet or database)
reasoning: PublicEngagementCoverage Scale requires >= 5 stakeholder groups engaged with documented feedback response. This Meter is the CAP 1616 audit. Accuracy is medium (stakeholder adequacy is subjective). Precision is medium. Cost is low (desk audit). Speed is high. Credibility is medium because it's documentary evidence; actual community satisfaction is harder to measure. Confidence: 7.
